profile.name: Alex Chen
profile.title: Biology Graduate | Data-Driven Science Communicator
profile.bio: I'm passionate about exploring the intersection of experimental science and computational analysis. This portfolio showcases my journey through research, the skills I've developed, and where my interests are leading me.

items.caption: Lab Setup
items.takeaway: Temperature-controlled incubators running parallel cultures. This taught me how much of good science is really just good logistics — labeling, timing, and consistency.

items.caption: Culture Plates — Day 7
items.takeaway: Visible colony differences across temperature conditions. First time I really understood how sensitive biological systems are to small environmental changes.

items.caption: Growth Curve Analysis
items.takeaway: Plotting OD600 readings over time. This is where I started teaching myself Python for data visualization instead of relying on Excel alone.

items.caption: Lab Notebook
items.takeaway: Daily handwritten logs. Learned the hard way why timestamping every observation matters when you're trying to reconstruct what happened three weeks later.

items.caption: Automation Script Interface
items.takeaway: My first real Python tool built for someone other than myself. Learned that writing code people other than you will use is a completely different skill than writing code for a class assignment.

items.caption: Before / After Data Cleanup
items.takeaway: Raw plate reader exports vs. standardized output. This project taught me that most 'data analysis' time is actually just data cleaning time.

items.caption: System Demo (2 min)
items.takeaway: Walkthrough of the automated report generation. Recording this forced me to explain my own code clearly, which honestly improved the code itself.

items.caption: Generated Weekly Report
items.takeaway: Used by 3 lab members. Small tool, but seeing someone else rely on something I built was a genuine turning point in how I saw my own skills.

items.caption: Workshop Setup
items.takeaway: Presenting antibiotic resistance concepts to high schoolers. Learned to strip jargon out of my explanations without dumbing down the actual science.

items.caption: Hands-On Microscopy
items.takeaway: Students identifying bacterial morphology under microscopes. Watching confusion turn into understanding in real time is what pointed me toward science communication as a career interest.

items.caption: Workshop Excerpt (3 min)
items.takeaway: Recorded explanation of resistance mechanisms. My first time seeing myself teach on camera — humbling, but useful for improving pacing and clarity.

items.caption: Workshop Cohort
items.takeaway: Five sessions, five different groups. Each one needed a slightly different approach, which taught me to read a room rather than run a fixed script.

researchProjects.title: Microbial Growth Kinetics Under Temperature Stress
researchProjects.context: A 12-week independent research project investigating how incubation temperature affects bacterial growth rate, run as part of a university research module. The goal was to characterize growth curves across five temperature conditions using optical density readings.
researchProjects.approach: Designed a sampling schedule across 50+ culture samples, took OD600 readings every 2 hours during the exponential phase, and used Python (pandas, matplotlib) to fit growth curves and compare rate constants across conditions.
researchProjects.challenges: My first two weeks of data were unusable — I hadn't controlled for incubator door-opening frequency, which introduced temperature fluctuations that swamped the actual signal. I had to redesign the sampling protocol and restart data collection, which cost me nearly a month.
researchProjects.learned: This was less a lesson in microbiology and more a lesson in experimental control: the variable you don't think to control is usually the one that ruins your data. It also taught me to build in a short pilot run before committing to a full data collection schedule, rather than assuming my first design would work.

researchProjects.title: Lab Data Automation Tool
researchProjects.context: Our lab was manually copying plate reader output into spreadsheets every week — a slow, error-prone process. I proposed and built a Python script to automate parsing, cleaning, and report generation.
researchProjects.approach: Built a command-line Python tool using pandas to parse raw plate reader exports, flag anomalous readings automatically, and generate a formatted weekly PDF summary for the lab.
researchProjects.challenges: The first version broke constantly because I'd only tested it against my own data exports, and other lab members' plate reader software produced slightly different file formats. I underestimated how much 'real world' input data varies compared to the clean examples I built and tested with.
researchProjects.learned: Robust code has to handle inputs you didn't anticipate, not just the ones you tested. I also learned the value of asking future users what their actual files look like before writing a single line of code, rather than after.

researchProjects.title: RNA-seq Analysis Exploration
researchProjects.status: Incomplete — did not reach a working pipeline
researchProjects.context: A self-directed attempt to learn RNA-seq differential expression analysis using R and Bioconductor, motivated by wanting to understand genomics workflows outside of coursework.
researchProjects.approach: Worked through public tutorials using DESeq2 on a sample dataset, aiming to eventually run the same pipeline on a small dataset from a professor's lab.
researchProjects.challenges: I underestimated how much background I was missing in R's data structures and Bioconductor's object conventions. I got stuck for several weeks on formatting my count matrix correctly and never got a full pipeline running end-to-end on real data before the term ended and my time ran out.
researchProjects.learned: Even though I didn't finish, I came away with a real understanding of what a differential expression workflow actually involves — normalization, dispersion estimation, multiple testing correction — which I couldn't have gotten from just reading about it. It also taught me to scope self-directed projects more realistically, and that an honest 'I got this far and here's exactly where I got stuck' is more useful to show than pretending a project went smoothly.

journalEntries.title: Why I keep coming back to science communication
journalEntries.content: I've noticed a pattern: whenever I have free time, I gravitate toward explaining things rather than just doing them. After finishing the growth kinetics project, I wanted to write it up for people who weren't in the field, not just file it away as a report. That instinct is starting to feel less like a hobby and more like a direction.

journalEntries.title: Reading outside my field on purpose
journalEntries.content: Started deliberately reading science journalism instead of only papers in my subfield — partly to see how good writers translate dense results into something a general reader can follow. It's changed how I write my own lab reports, for the better I think.

journalEntries.title: What the failed RNA-seq attempt actually gave me
journalEntries.content: I spent two months on a project that never fully worked, and my first instinct was to be embarrassed about it. But writing up what I learned for my research portfolio made me realize the getting-stuck part is where most of the actual learning happened. I'm trying to be less precious about only showcasing finished things.

journalEntries.title: Teaching microbiology to 16-year-olds
journalEntries.content: Ran my first workshop this week. I over-prepared slides and under-prepared for questions — the students asked things I hadn't considered, which was more useful than anything in my planning. I want to do more of this.
